did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used YOLOv8, a convolutional neural network (deep learning) model, for multi-class skin lesion detection and classification of benign and malignant skin cancer using the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from training and evaluating five YOLOv8 model variants on the HAM10000 dataset for multi-class skin lesion detection. It includes a methodology section detailing data preprocessing, augmentation, and model training parameters, along with a results section reporting specific performance metrics (precision, recall, mAP50) and a conclusion based on these new findings. Although published in a conference proceedings (SIML), it reports original technical research rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Performance Evaluation of YOLOv8 Models for Multi-Class Skin Lesion Detection from Dermoscopic Images
journal: 2025 International Conference on Smart Computing, IoT and Machine Learning (SIML)
brief_summary: This study evaluated five YOLOv8 model variants (n, s, m, l, x) for multi-class skin lesion detection using the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset. To address class imbalance, the researchers applied undersampling and data augmentation techniques, resizing images to 224x224 pixels. The models were trained for 100 epochs and assessed using precision, recall, and mAP50 metrics on a dedicated test set. The results indicated that YOLOv8s achieved the highest overall performance with an mAP50 of 0.890, precision of 0.840, and recall of 0.857. For specific tasks, YOLOv8s was optimal for benign lesion detection, while YOLOv8m showed the best sensitivity for malignant lesions. The findings highlight the potential of YOLOv8s as a reliable tool for early skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign lesion class in the HAM10000 dataset; 6,705 images in original distribution, undersampled to equal class counts.
Malignant lesion class in the HAM10000 dataset; 1,113 images in original distribution.
Benign lesion class in the HAM10000 dataset; 1,099 images in original distribution.
Malignant lesion class in the HAM10000 dataset; 514 images in original distribution.
Malignant/pre-malignant lesion class in the HAM10000 dataset; 327 images in original distribution.
Benign lesion class in the HAM10000 dataset; 142 images in original distribution.
Benign lesion class in the HAM10000 dataset; 115 images in original distribution, used as the minority class for undersampling.

all_task_types.target: Bounding boxes and class labels for skin lesions in dermoscopic images.
all_task_types.details: The study uses YOLOv8 models for multi-class skin lesion detection. The paper explicitly states the selection of an object detection model over a classification model to provide 'not only the type of skin lesion but also its precise location within the image.' The models predict bounding boxes and classify lesions into seven categories (NV, MEL, BKL, BCC, AKIEC, VASC, DF) using metrics like mAP50, precision, and recall.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset comprising seven classes of skin lesions. The original dataset contained 10,015 images with significant class imbalance (ranging from 115 to 6,705 images per class). For this study, the dataset was processed using undersampling to equalize class counts to the minority class (Dermatofibroma, 115 images), followed by data augmentation (rotations, shifts, zoom, flips) resulting in a final set of 2,695 images. Images were resized to 224x224 pixels and labeled with bounding boxes using Roboflow.

use.training: Used for training five YOLOv8 model variants (n, s, m, l, x) for 100 epochs with a batch size of 16.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training phase (10% of the processed dataset).
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (testing phase) representing 10% of the processed dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between YOLOv8 variants were based on descriptive metrics (Precision, Recall, mAP50, F1-Score) without statistical significance testing.

training_phase.description: Performance metrics during the training phase over 100 epochs.

YOLOv8n.mAP50: Not explicitly stated as a single training value in text, but Table II shows test mAP50 of 0.885. Text states YOLOv8s had highest mAP50 of 0.890.

YOLOv8m.mAP50: Not explicitly stated as a single training value in text.

YOLOv8l.mAP50: Not explicitly stated as a single training value in text.

YOLOv8x.mAP50: Not explicitly stated as a single training value in text.

training_phase.notes: YOLOv8l achieved the highest precision (0.858) during training. YOLOv8s achieved the highest recall (0.899) during training. YOLOv8s achieved the highest mAP50 (0.890) during training.

testing_phase.description: Performance metrics on the dedicated test set (10% of the processed dataset).
testing_phase.notes: For benign lesion detection, YOLOv8s recorded the highest precision (0.895 for AKIEC class, 0.800 mean) and mAP50 (0.951 for AKIEC class, 0.844 mean). YOLOv8l had the highest recall for benign lesions (0.928 for NV class, 0.839 mean). For malignant lesion detection, YOLOv8m demonstrated the highest precision (0.802 mean) and mAP50 (0.851 mean). YOLOv8n exhibited the highest recall for malignant lesions (0.857 mean). Overall, YOLOv8s showed the best balanced performance with the highest overall mAP50 (0.890), precision (0.840), and recall (0.857).

architecture_details.neck: Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) or Path Aggregation Network (PANet)
architecture_details.heads: Detection head predicting bounding boxes, object classes, and confidence scores; Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) used for post-processing
architecture_details.model_type: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) object detection model

data_augmentation: Nearest neighbor filling for empty areas

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Bounding boxes, class labels, and confidence scores

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not reported (Paper notes that variants differ in parameter counts but does not provide specific numbers)

preprocessing_techniques: Undersampling to equalize class counts to the minority class (Dermatofibroma, 115 images)

Data augmentation using ImageDataGenerator from Keras TensorFlow including rotations up to 180°, horizontal and vertical shifts by 10%, zoom up to 10%, horizontal and vertical flips, and nearest neighbor filling for empty areas

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to equalize class counts to the minority class (Dermatofibroma, 115 images) to address class imbalance before augmentation.
Nearest neighbor method used to fill empty areas created by transformations.

conclusions: YOLOv8s demonstrated the highest overall performance for multi-class skin lesion detection, achieving an mAP50 of 0.890, precision of 0.840, and recall of 0.857 on the HAM10000 dataset.
For the specific task of detecting benign skin lesions, YOLOv8s is the most suitable model, achieving a precision of 0.895 and an mAP50 of 0.951.

For the specific task of detecting malignant skin lesions, YOLOv8m is the optimal choice, demonstrating the highest precision (0.802) and mAP50 (0.851), while YOLOv8n showed the highest recall (0.857) for identifying malignant cases.
The study highlights the potential of YOLOv8 models, particularly YOLOv8s, to enhance diagnostic accuracy and support early detection efforts for both benign and malignant skin cancers.

The authors acknowledge limitations regarding generalizability and propose future work involving more diverse, private datasets and the development of an integrated user interface for real-time clinical application.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by undersampling majority classes to match the minority class (Dermatofibroma, 115 images), which may artificially inflate performance metrics and reduce the model's ability to generalize to real-world imbalanced distributions.

The dataset was split into subsets with a maximum of 400 images per subset before augmentation, a non-standard splitting procedure that deviates from typical random splits and may introduce selection bias.

The dataset was augmented using Keras ImageDataGenerator (rotations, shifts, zoom, flips) after the train/validation/test split, which is a correct approach to prevent data leakage, but the resulting test set (266 images) is very small for robust evaluation.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, limiting the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between YOLOv8 variants, relying solely on descriptive metrics.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability and proposes future work using diverse, private datasets, indicating current results may not reflect clinical reality.
The input image size was reduced to 224x224 pixels, which the authors note may decrease accuracy for small objects or complex details, potentially impacting detection performance for smaller lesions.
The paper does not report the optimizer, loss function, or learning rate used during training, limiting reproducibility.
The study focuses on object detection rather than classification, providing bounding boxes which is clinically relevant for localization, but the evaluation is limited to a single public dataset.
